steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims congestion rose during the 1990s in both very large cities (NYC) and smaller areas, with smaller areas less affected throughout. The correct choice must show: (1) a persistent gap with the large city higher every year, and (2) upward trends in both cities over time.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Even though the amount of traffic delay per commuter per year was greater in the Albuquerque, New Mexico, area than in the New York City, New York, area throughout the period from 1990 to 2000, the amounts were nearly identical in 1996.': This says Albuquerque exceeded NYC, which contradicts the graph where NYC is consistently higher. ✗
  • Choice B — 'While the annual number of hours of traffic delay per commuter was always higher in the New York City, New York, area than in the Boise, Idaho, area for each year between 1990 and 2000, the amount of traffic delay rose in both areas during this period.': NYC is consistently above Boise every year (satisfying the persistent gap) and both trend upward across the decade (satisfying the worsening claim). Both parts of the student's claim are supported. ✓
  • Choice C — 'In 1992, the amount of traffic delay in the New York City, New York, area was less than 30 hours per commuter per year.': A single year's snapshot shows neither a gap nor a trend. Does not support the full claim. ✗
  • Choice D — 'In at least one of the four urban areas shown, the amount of traffic delay was less than 20 hours per commuter per year at one point between 1990 and 2000.': Noting a low value in one area at one point says nothing about overall trends or city-size comparisons. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
